hero.subtitle: A capability-based microkernel operating system written in Rust
hero.description: The kernel is a small, safe arbiter. Every resource has a clear type and is reached through an explicit, unforgeable capability. Security is least-privilege by construction.

about.title: About
about.intro: {project} is a modern operating system written from scratch in Rust. It is built around a small, memory-safe microkernel and a typed object / capability model. System services and drivers run as isolated, restartable components that talk to each other over stable, typed contracts. The project is open source and distributed under the {license}.

microkernel.title: Memory-safe microkernel
microkernel.description: The kernel is a small, safe arbiter written in Rust. Memory safety comes from the language itself rather than from discipline, and the kernel stays minimal - everything that can live outside of it does.

capabilities.title: Capability-based security
capabilities.description: Every resource has a clear type and is reached through an explicit, unforgeable capability that carries its own rights. Least privilege is enforced by construction, not by convention - and capabilities can be revoked at any time.

services.title: Isolated, restartable services
services.description: System services and drivers run as isolated components outside the kernel. A crashed driver or service is restarted without taking the system down, keeping faults contained.

contracts.title: Typed contracts (LSIDL)
contracts.description: Components talk to each other over stable, typed contracts described in LSIDL - the {project} Interface Definition Language - from which the wire codec, clients, servers and documentation are generated.

liberfs.title: LiberFS filesystem
liberfs.description: The native filesystem is copy-on-write, extent-mapped, checksummed and supports snapshots - designed for integrity and consistency from the first block.

smp.title: SMP from the ground up
smp.description: The system is designed for multi-core hardware from the very beginning - not as an afterthought. Scheduling and kernel data structures are SMP-aware by design.

download.title: Download
download.description: Releases are published on GitHub as they become available - along with the full source code.

documentation.title: Documentation
documentation.description: Design documents and technical references, maintained together with the source code.

concept.title: Concept
concept.description: The {project} design document: object and capability model, IPC, services, and the roadmap.

threatModel.title: Threat model
threatModel.description: What the system defends against, where the security boundaries are, and how each boundary is turned into an executable check.

lsidl.title: LSIDL
lsidl.description: The {project} Interface Definition Language: the language services are described in, from which the wire codec, clients, servers, and docs are generated.

systemInterfaces.title: System interfaces
systemInterfaces.description: The generated reference for the liber:system package: every record, enum and service interface, as defined in the LSIDL sources.

liberfs.title: LiberFS
liberfs.description: The native filesystem: the copy-on-write, extent-mapped, checksummed and snapshotting on-disk format, and how it compares with FAT, NTFS, ext4, XFS, Btrfs and ZFS.

debugging.title: Debugging
debugging.description: The debugging toolbox: the lab harness that drives a live instance, timing and tracing, GDB, packet capture, and the test suite.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: How to build {project} from source and run it in a virtual machine.

repository.title: Source code
repository.description: The full {project} source code, issue tracker and development history on GitHub.

comparison.title: Comparison
comparison.description: How {project} differs from a traditional Unix-like operating system such as Linux.

kernel.libersystem: A small capability-based microkernel: only memory, scheduling, IPC, capabilities and safe hardware access.
kernel.linux: A monolithic kernel: drivers, filesystems and network stacks run inside the kernel.

systemApi.libersystem: One typed object / capability API with binary, CBOR, JSON and CLI representations.
systemApi.linux: POSIX syscalls plus textual pseudo-files (/proc, /sys, /dev) and ioctl calls.

security.libersystem: Capabilities: a process holds only the rights it was explicitly given. No root, no ambient authority.
security.linux: Users, groups and a root account that can do anything.

storage.libersystem: Explicit volumes: a path always belongs to exactly one volume and can never silently touch a different disk.
storage.linux: A single global directory tree where disks are attached via mount points.

resources.libersystem: Every resource is a typed object with a clear interface.
resources.linux: "Everything is a file": processes, devices and settings blended into untyped byte streams.

drivers.libersystem: Isolated userspace services: a crashed driver is restarted without taking the system down.
drivers.linux: Mostly inside the kernel: a driver crash can bring the whole system down.

memorySafety.libersystem: Guaranteed by the Rust language itself.
memorySafety.linux: Written in C: memory safety depends on the discipline of the programmer.

applications.libersystem: Sandboxed WebAssembly/WASI components: one artifact runs on x86-64, ARM64 and RISC-V.
applications.linux: Native binaries compiled per architecture, sandboxing is opt-in.

compatibility.libersystem: An optional userspace translation layer (planned), never inside the kernel.
compatibility.linux: POSIX is the native, primary API.
